topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sudden emptiness where the towering oak once stood felt like a physical blow. Sarah stood at the edge of the clearing, her fingers tracing the ragged, splintered stump where her childhood ambition had rooted itself. She remembered the painstaking effort of those small hands, the whispered promises to the sapling, the sheer, hopeful belief that she could coax something magnificent from the earth. Now, only debris remained, a stark, brutal testament to a life that had been swiftly erased. A tightness coiled in her chest, a familiar, sour knot tightening with every glance toward the distant, thriving canopy of the neighboring woods—a canopy that seemed to boast of effortless, established grandeur. She kicked at a loose piece of bark, the sound sharp in the silence, a small, frustrated snarl escaping her lips.

[story 2]

Mark walked the familiar path, his steps heavy and dragging against the damp soil. He stopped abruptly when the familiar silhouette vanished. Where the shade of his little sapling used to be, there was only a freshly cleared patch of dirt, the earth looking raw and exposed under the midday sun. A deep, hollow ache settled beneath his ribs, a feeling of being left behind, of having invested so much care only to watch it all vanish. He ran a hand over his own forearm, feeling the slight tremor in his hand. He watched a group of older teenagers pass by, their laughter echoing as they strolled beneath the mature trees, their presence radiating an easy confidence he simply didn't possess. He clenched his jaw, the muscles in his neck corded with a sharp, internal tension.

[story 3]

"Gone," he murmured, the word tasting like ash in his mouth. David knelt beside the barren ground, his gaze fixed on the exposed roots, now brittle and severed. He felt a strange, hot flush creeping up his neck, a burning sensation that had nothing to do with the sun. He remembered the pride he’d felt when the first leaves unfurled, the sheer, unadulterated joy of creation. That memory now felt tainted, overlaid with a bitter residue. He looked across the field where the established oaks stood, their branches thick and unwavering, reaching for the sky with an assuredness that made his own small, recent endeavor seem utterly insignificant. He stood up slowly, his shoulders slumped, the air seeming too thick to draw in.
